cue: How do you say something will never happen?

es.meaning: Never; on a day that will not come.
es.example: ¿Que él pida perdón? Cuando las ranas críen pelo.

it.meaning: Never; on a day that will not come.
it.example: Che lui chieda scusa? Quando gli asini voleranno.

bridge.note: A competition in impossibility, and each language picks a different kind. Spanish asks a frog to grow HAIR — an absurdity of biology, a small wet animal acquiring what it could never have. Italian asks a donkey to FLY — an absurdity of physics, the heaviest, most earthbound animal leaving the ground. English splits the difference with a flying pig. Three languages, three ways of saying the universe would have to change first.

interference.es_to_it: Frogs and hair get a blank look in Italy; the impossible thing there is a donkey in the air. Also common: alle calende greche.
interference.it_to_es: Flying donkeys are not the Spanish formula — the frog must grow hair. Mexico also says 'cuando San Juan agache el dedo'.

review.notes: ES side discovered in a Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; every gloss and example here authored fresh). IT counterpart chosen by Claude from recall — UNVERIFIED. Biology-vs-physics framing; the MX San Juan variant is worth a region note if the reviewer confirms it.
